children.name: Sorex
children.description: Red-toothed shrews of the Holarctic — the most species-rich shrew genus, characterised by red-tipped teeth coated in iron-containing pigment that strengthens the tooth enamel. Shrews have one of the highest metabolic rates of any mammal: the common shrew must eat every 2–3 hours or die. Their hearts beat up to 1,320 times per minute and their metabolic rate is 50 times that of a human per unit body weight. In autumn, some species undergo Dehnel's phenomenon — the brain, skull, and internal organs actually shrink to reduce the energy cost of survival through winter, then regrow in spring.

children.name: Sorex araneus
children.description: Among the most abundant mammals in European woodland and hedgerow. Must consume 90% of its body weight daily. Undergoes dramatic Dehnel shrinkage in winter. Lives for just 14–19 months.

children.name: Sorex minutus
children.description: Weighs 3–6 g — one of the smallest land mammals in the world. Ireland's only native land insectivore. Must eat its own body weight every few hours. Astonishingly fierce for its size, taking invertebrates much larger than itself.

children.name: Sorex coronatus
children.description: A European species distinguished from S. araneus by white-tipped teeth.

children.name: Sorex alpinus
children.description: A high-elevation species of the Alps, Carpathians and Balkans.

children.name: Sorex cinereus
children.description: The most widely distributed North American shrew; found from Alaska to the Appalachians.

children.name: Sorex palustris
children.description: A semi-aquatic shrew that dives for aquatic invertebrates; has stiff hairs on hind feet for paddling.

children.name: Sorex vagrans
children.description: A common western North American shrew with a broad habitat tolerance.

children.name: Sorex monticolus
children.description: Found in western montane forests; one of the most abundant small mammals in the Rockies.

children.name: Sorex dispar
children.description: A rare shrew of talus slopes in the Appalachians; named for its proportionally long tail.

children.name: Sorex unguiculatus
children.description: A Japanese and Sakhalin species with enlarged front claws for digging.

children.name: Sorex gracillimus
children.description: One of the smallest Sorex; found in Hokkaido and the Russian Far East.

children.name: Sorex isodon
children.description: A boreal shrew with uniform tooth pigmentation; ranges from Scandinavia to Kamchatka.

children.name: Sorex rohri
children.description: One of the few African Sorex; found in highland forests of Tanzania.

children.name: Crocidura
children.description: White-toothed shrews — the largest genus of any mammal with over 180 species. Unlike red-toothed shrews, their teeth lack iron pigment and remain white throughout life. White-toothed shrews are found across Africa, Europe, and Asia, and are among the most diverse mammals on Earth. Several island species show dramatic gigantism (Crocidura of the Canary Islands) or dwarfism compared to mainland relatives. Young white-toothed shrews form 'caravans' — the first infant grips its mother's tail, subsequent ones grip the preceding sibling, forming a chain that maintains family cohesion.

children.name: Crocidura russula
children.description: The commonest shrew in many parts of southern Europe. More tolerant of human habitation than red-toothed shrews. Young form characteristic caravans, each gripping the rump of the animal in front.

children.name: Crocidura suaveolens
children.description: Found across a vast range from western Europe to Japan. On Scilly and the Channel Islands it is the only shrew species. Island populations often show differences in body size from mainland forms.

children.name: Crocidura leucodon
children.description: Distinguished by its sharp white-grey dorsal and pale ventral colour boundary.

children.name: Crocidura olivieri
children.description: The largest Crocidura; weighs up to 50 g; found across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Crocidura attila
children.description: A Southeast Asian species named for its aggressive predatory behaviour for a shrew.

children.name: Crocidura goliath
children.description: A very large Central African rainforest shrew weighing up to 40 g.

children.name: Crocidura lasiura
children.description: The largest Palearctic Crocidura; found in the Russian Far East and Korea.

children.name: Crocidura cyanea
children.description: A small southern African species with a bluish-grey sheen to its fur.

children.name: Crocidura dolichura
children.description: A Central African species with a notably long tail relative to body length.

children.name: Crocidura whiteheadi
children.description: A Bornean endemic; part of the diverse Southeast Asian Crocidura radiation.

children.name: Crocidura ramona
children.description: An Israeli endemic restricted to the Negev desert highlands.

children.name: Crocidura monax
children.description: Named for its unusual reduced tooth count; found in Tanzania's Eastern Arc Mountains.

children.name: Neomys
children.description: Water shrews — the largest shrews in Europe, specialised for aquatic hunting with fringes of stiff hairs on their hind feet and tail that act as paddles. They are venomous: saliva from submandibular glands contains blarina-toxin, a neurotoxin that immobilises earthworms, frogs, and small fish, allowing the shrew to store living but paralysed prey. Water shrews can dive to the bottom of streams and walk along the substrate hunting for invertebrates, fish eggs, and small fish. Their dense fur traps air bubbles that make them highly buoyant, so they must actively swim downward.

children.name: Neomys fodiens
children.description: Europe's largest shrew. Mildly venomous — a bite to a human causes localised pain and swelling. Swims with a distinctive bubble coating. Requires clean, fast-flowing streams with good invertebrate populations.

children.name: Neomys anomalus
children.description: A smaller relative of the Eurasian water shrew with less developed fringing hairs.

children.name: Neomys milleri
children.description: Named after the naturalist; found in the Balkan peninsula and Asia Minor.

children.name: Suncus
children.description: Suncus shrews, including the Etruscan shrew — the world's smallest mammal by mass. The Etruscan shrew (Suncus etruscus) weighs just 1.8–2.5 grams — lighter than a 2p coin. Its heart beats 1,200 times per minute at rest and up to 1,511 times per minute during activity, the fastest heart rate of any known mammal. To fuel this metabolism it must eat almost continuously, consuming prey larger than itself. Despite its tiny size, it maintains a body temperature of 37°C and must eat every 30 minutes. The musk shrew (Suncus murinus) is a commensal species that has spread worldwide with human trade.

children.name: Suncus etruscus
children.description: The world's smallest mammal by mass at 1.8–2.5 g (kitti's hog-nosed bat is smaller by skull dimensions). Heart rate can exceed 1,500 bpm. Found in warm, dry scrubland around the Mediterranean.

children.name: Suncus murinus
children.description: A large shrew that has spread across the tropics with human trade; produces a strong musky odour.

children.name: Suncus fellescens
children.description: An endemic Sulawesi species; one of the largest Suncus.

children.name: Suncus infinitestimulus
children.description: Endemic to Socotra Island; one of the least-known shrew species.

children.name: Suncus lixus
children.description: A Central African Suncus found in wetland margins and grassy savannas.

children.name: Blarina
children.description: Medium-sized, robust shrews of North America with distinctive short tails. They are among the few venomous mammals — their saliva contains a neurotoxin that paralyses earthworms and small vertebrates.

children.name: Blarina brevicauda
children.description: The most venomous shrew; its bite carries a neurotoxin capable of causing swelling and pain in humans that can last for days.

children.name: Blarina carolinensis
children.description: A smaller southern relative; uses venom to immobilise prey for later consumption.

children.name: Blarina hylophaga
children.description: A central US species separated from B. brevicauda on chromosomal and genetic grounds.
